name: Artin's Theorem on Primitive Roots

phase_0.boundary_condition: The cyclic group (ℤ/nℤ)* and its generators under modular multiplication

VINIT.justification: Before testing whether g is a primitive root mod n, g exists as a bare element with no properties assigned

TANCH.chosen_element: the modulus n and the group (ℤ/nℤ)*
TANCH.justification: The boundary that contains all possible primitive roots; n fixes the multiplicative group and its order φ(n)

AFWD.chosen_element: exponentiation g^k mod n for k = 1, 2, ..., φ(n)
AFWD.justification: Forward morphism: successive powers of g generate elements of the group, advancing toward full coverage

AREV.chosen_element: reduction of exponent k modulo φ(n) (Euler's theorem)
AREV.justification: Reverse morphism: g^φ(n) ≡ 1 (mod n) collapses the cycle back; descent from arbitrary k to canonical representative

CLINK.chosen_element: composition of successive exponentiations: g^(k₁) · g^(k₂) = g^(k₁+k₂)
CLINK.justification: Sequential chaining of group operations; multiplication in the exponent composes the morphisms

IMSCRIB.justification: Self-reference: 1 is the fixed point of the group; g^φ(n) = 1 means the cycle recognizes itself

FSPLIT.chosen_element: partition of divisors of φ(n) into two branches: proper divisors d | φ(n), d < φ(n) vs. d = φ(n)
FSPLIT.justification: Splits the order-testing problem: either g^d ≡ 1 (mod n) for some proper divisor d (failure branch), or g^φ(n) is the first power equaling 1 (success branch)

FFUSE.chosen_element: reconstitution of the full order test: if no proper divisor d of φ(n) satisfies g^d ≡ 1, then ord(g) = φ(n), confirming g is a primitive root
FFUSE.justification: Fuses the two branches back: the absence of early collapse (F-branch empty) combined with g^φ(n) ≡ 1 (T-branch) recovers the original candidate g as a verified primitive root

EVALT.chosen_element: g^φ(n) ≡ 1 (mod n) AND no proper divisor d of φ(n) satisfies g^d ≡ 1
EVALT.justification: Affirmative evaluation: g is a primitive root; the order of g equals φ(n)

EVALF.chosen_element: there exists a proper divisor d of φ(n) such that g^d ≡ 1 (mod n)
EVALF.justification: Negative evaluation: g is not a primitive root; its order is strictly less than φ(n)

ENGAGR.chosen_element: the case where n has no primitive roots (e.g., n = 2^k for k ≥ 3, or n = 2·p^k for odd prime p)
ENGAGR.justification: Paradice: the theorem holds (primitive roots exist for certain n), yet for other n they do not exist; both the existence and non-existence are simultaneously true in the domain of all n

IFIX.chosen_element: the list of all primitive roots mod n once fully enumerated and verified
IFIX.justification: Permanent record: once a primitive root g is confirmed, it is appended to the canonical list; this record is irreversible and forms the witness to Artin's theorem

phase_2.split_input: candidate g and the set of all divisors of φ(n)
split_outputs: T-branch: divisor d = φ(n) (g^φ(n) ≡ 1 mod n)
F-branch: proper divisors d | φ(n), d < φ(n) (test if g^d ≡ 1 mod n)
phase_2.fuse_element: FFUSE: reconstitute the order of g
phase_2.fuse_result: if F-branch is empty (no proper divisor d satisfies g^d ≡ 1), then ord(g) = φ(n), confirming g as primitive root; the input candidate g is recovered as verified

phase_3.void_description: 00 — g is an uninitialized candidate; no order has been computed; no divisors of φ(n) have been tested
phase_3.true_description: 01 — g is confirmed as a primitive root; ord(g) = φ(n); g generates the full multiplicative group (ℤ/nℤ)*
phase_3.false_description: 10 — g is not a primitive root; there exists a proper divisor d of φ(n) such that g^d ≡ 1 (mod n); ord(g) < φ(n)
phase_3.both_description: 11 — paradice: the theorem asserts primitive roots exist for n ∈ {1, 2, 4, p^k, 2p^k} (p odd prime), yet for all other n they do not; both existence and non-existence are held simultaneously in the universal domain of all moduli

steps.domain_action: initialize candidate g with no properties; void state (00)
establish the modulus n and the multiplicative group (ℤ/nℤ)* with order φ(n); this is the boundary containing all possible primitive roots
compute φ(n) using Euler's totient function; forward morphism toward the target order
compose the factorization of φ(n) into prime powers; chain the divisor structure
recognize the identity element 1 ≡ g^0 (mod n); self-reference point of the group
branch on the divisors of φ(n); split into T-branch (d = φ(n)) and F-branch (proper divisors d < φ(n))
on T-branch, compute g^φ(n) mod n; forward exponentiation toward the full order
on T-branch, evaluate g^φ(n) ≡ 1 (mod n) as true; Euler's theorem guarantees this
on F-branch, for each proper divisor d of φ(n), compute g^d mod n; reverse descent through divisor lattice
on F-branch, test if g^d ≡ 1 (mod n) for any proper divisor d; if true, g is not a primitive root
fuse the two branches; if F-branch found no proper divisor d with g^d ≡ 1, then ord(g) = φ(n)
final affirmative: g is a primitive root; ord(g) = φ(n); g generates (ℤ/nℤ)*
record g in the canonical list of primitive roots mod n; append-only, permanent witness
compose the verification: if g is primitive, then g^k generates all elements of (ℤ/nℤ)* as k ranges over 1 to φ(n)
self-reference: the set of all primitive roots mod n is closed under the group operation; it is self-recognizing
paradice state: Artin's theorem asserts primitive roots exist for n ∈ {1, 2, 4, p^k, 2p^k}, yet for all other n they do not; both existence and non-existence are simultaneously true
reverse morphism: given a primitive root g, the inverse g^(-1) ≡ g^(φ(n)-1) (mod n) is also a primitive root; descent through the group structure
compose the orbit structure: if g is primitive, then {g^1, g^2, ..., g^φ(n)} = (ℤ/nℤ)*; chaining all powers recovers the full group
forward morphism: the exponent k advances from 1 to φ(n); each step generates a new group element
identity recognition: g^φ(n) ≡ 1 (mod n) is the fixed point; the cycle closes on itself
record the complete orbit of g; permanent enumeration of all group elements generated by g
close the boundary: the set of all primitive roots mod n, together with the group structure, forms a complete system; Artin's theorem is the boundary condition

phase_5.compiler_frontend: Euler's totient function φ(n) and the factorization of φ(n) into prime powers; these translate the modulus n into the order of the multiplicative group
phase_5.ipc_mechanism: Modular exponentiation g^k mod n; the communication protocol between candidate g and the group (ℤ/nℤ)* is repeated squaring and reduction
phase_5.memory_mechanism: The divisor lattice of φ(n) is stored; for each divisor d, the result of g^d mod n is cached; the canonical list of primitive roots is appended to IFIX
phase_5.scheduler_mechanism: Iterate through all divisors d of φ(n) in increasing order; test g^d ≡ 1 (mod n) for each; if no proper divisor satisfies this, g is primitive
phase_5.alfs_store: The Artin conjecture and its proof: primitive roots exist for n ∈ {1, 2, 4, p^k, 2p^k} where p is an odd prime; this is the canonical reference for the domain

phase_6.cycle_cost: Each modular exponentiation g^d mod n costs O(log d · log² n) bit operations; testing all divisors of φ(n) costs O(τ(φ(n)) · log φ(n) · log² n) where τ is the divisor count function
phase_6.pre_cycle_state: Candidate g is uninitialized; no divisors of φ(n) have been tested; the group (ℤ/nℤ)* is defined but its generators are unknown
phase_6.post_cycle_state: g is classified as either a primitive root (ord(g) = φ(n)) or not (ord(g) < φ(n)); the divisor lattice of φ(n) has been traversed; the canonical list of primitive roots is extended by one entry (if g is primitive)
phase_6.delta_s_verdict: ΔS ≈ 0 — the cycle is entropy-conservative because the Frobenius constraint FFUSE(FSPLIT(g)) = g holds: splitting g into the two branches (T: full order, F: proper divisors) and fusing them back recovers g with its order fully determined; no information is lost; the group structure is cyclic and deterministic, so the entropy of the system remains constant modulo the irreversible append to IFIX
